title: Route Congestion Heat Windows
description: Find top three non-overlapping windows of size k with highest congestion score

examples.explanation: Step 1: Generate all windows of size 2. Windows: (score, start, end): (3,0,1), (5,1,2), (7,2,3), (9,3,4), (11,4,5), (13,5,6), (15,6,7), (17,7,8). Step 2: Try combinations of 3 non-overlapping windows. Check combination windows (17,7,8), (15,6,7), (13,5,6): Overlap between (15,6,7) and (17,7,8) at index 7, invalid. Check combination (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (9,3,4): No overlap, total = 17+13+9 = 39. Check combination (15,6,7), (11,4,5), (7,2,3): No overlap, total = 15+11+7 = 33. Check combination (17,7,8), (11,4,5), (5,1,2): No overlap, total = 17+11+5 = 33. Check combination (13,5,6), (9,3,4), (5,1,2): No overlap, total = 13+9+5 = 27. Maximum found: 39 from windows (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (9,3,4). Wait, let me check more combinations. Actually, the best combination is (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (9,3,4) = 39. But let me verify all: Windows sorted by score: 17,15,13,11,9,7,5,3. Try (17,7,8), (15,6,7), (13,5,6): Overlap. (17,7,8), (15,6,7), (11,4,5): Overlap. (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (11,4,5): Overlap at 5-6 and 4-5? No, 5-6 and 4-5 are adjacent but not overlapping (4-5 ends at 5, 5-6 starts at 5, they share index 5? Actually window (4,5) covers indices 4,5. Window (5,6) covers indices 5,6. They share index 5, so they overlap. Invalid. (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (9,3,4): No overlap, total 39. (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (7,2,3): No overlap, total 37. (17,7,8), (11,4,5), (9,3,4): Overlap at 4-5 and 3-4? Share index 4, invalid. (15,6,7), (13,5,6), (11,4,5): Overlap. (15,6,7), (13,5,6), (9,3,4): Overlap at 6-7 and 5-6? Share index 6, invalid. (15,6,7), (11,4,5), (9,3,4): Overlap at 4-5 and 3-4? Share index 4, invalid. (15,6,7), (11,4,5), (7,2,3): No overlap, total 33. (13,5,6), (11,4,5), (9,3,4): Overlap. (13,5,6), (11,4,5), (7,2,3): Overlap at 5-6 and 4-5? Share index 5, invalid. (13,5,6), (9,3,4), (7,2,3): No overlap, total 29. Maximum is 39.

Step 1: Generate all windows of size 1. Windows: (10,0,0), (20,1,1), (30,2,2), (40,3,3), (50,4,4). Step 2: Find top 3 non-overlapping windows. Since k=1, windows are single indices, non-overlapping means different indices. Simply pick the three highest values: 50, 40, 30. Total = 50+40+30 = 120. Check combinations: (50,4,4), (40,3,3), (30,2,2): No overlap, total 120. Any other combination gives lower total.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(nk²)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible windows of size k, calculate congestion scores for each, then try all combinations of three non-overlapping windows to find the maximum total score. This approach is straightforward but extremely inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Top Three Non-Overlapping Windows
ladders.desc: Find top three non-overlapping windows of size k with highest total congestion score using brute force O(nk²) approach.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete problem solution using the brute force approach. The intuition is straightforward: generate all possible windows, calculate their congestion scores, then try every combination of three non-overlapping windows to find the maximum total score. While conceptually simple, this approach becomes computationally prohibitive for large inputs due to its O(nk²) time complexity. The algorithm works by first creating all possible windows of size k from the congestion array. For each window, we calculate the congestion score by summing all elements within that window. This gives us a list of windows with their scores and positions. Next, we generate all combinations of three windows from this list, filtering out combinations where windows overlap (share any indices). For each valid combination, we calculate the total score by summing the three window scores. We track the maximum total score found. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize empty list windows. 2. For i from 0 to n-k: a. Create window from indices i to i+k-1. b. Calculate score = sum(arr[i:i+k]). c. Store (score, start_index, end_index). 3. Initialize max_score = -infinity. 4. For all combinations of three windows from windows list: a. Check if windows are non-overlapping (no shared indices). b. If valid, calculate total = score1 + score2 + score3. c. Update max_score if total > max_score. 5. Return max_score. Time complexity: O(nk²) because generating windows takes O(nk) and checking combinations takes O(C(n,3)) ≈ O(n³), but with the window generation dominating. Space complexity: O(n) to store window information. Connection to L1: This level uses the window generation and score calculation from L1 as a key subroutine. Common mistakes include: not handling edge cases where n < 3k (impossible to find three non-overlapping windows), incorrect overlap checking, and inefficient combination generation. Real code pattern: Use nested loops for window generation, then triple nested loops for combination checking with overlap validation.

examples.trace: Step 1: Generate all windows of size 2. Windows: (score, start, end): (3,0,1), (5,1,2), (7,2,3), (9,3,4), (11,4,5), (13,5,6), (15,6,7), (17,7,8). Step 2: Try combinations of 3 non-overlapping windows. Check combination windows (17,7,8), (15,6,7), (13,5,6): Overlap between (15,6,7) and (17,7,8) at index 7, invalid. Check combination (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (9,3,4): No overlap, total = 17+13+9 = 39. Check combination (15,6,7), (11,4,5), (7,2,3): No overlap, total = 15+11+7 = 33. Check combination (17,7,8), (11,4,5), (5,1,2): No overlap, total = 17+11+5 = 33. Check combination (13,5,6), (9,3,4), (5,1,2): No overlap, total = 13+9+5 = 27. Maximum found: 39 from windows (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (9,3,4). Wait, let me check more combinations. Actually, the best combination is (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (9,3,4) = 39. But let me verify all: Windows sorted by score: 17,15,13,11,9,7,5,3. Try (17,7,8), (15,6,7), (13,5,6): Overlap. (17,7,8), (15,6,7), (11,4,5): Overlap. (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (11,4,5): Overlap at 5-6 and 4-5? No, 5-6 and 4-5 are adjacent but not overlapping (4-5 ends at 5, 5-6 starts at 5, they share index 5? Actually window (4,5) covers indices 4,5. Window (5,6) covers indices 5,6. They share index 5, so they overlap. Invalid. (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (9,3,4): No overlap, total 39. (17,7,8), (13,5,6), (7,2,3): No overlap, total 37. (17,7,8), (11,4,5), (9,3,4): Overlap at 4-5 and 3-4? Share index 4, invalid. (15,6,7), (13,5,6), (11,4,5): Overlap. (15,6,7), (13,5,6), (9,3,4): Overlap at 6-7 and 5-6? Share index 6, invalid. (15,6,7), (11,4,5), (9,3,4): Overlap at 4-5 and 3-4? Share index 4, invalid. (15,6,7), (11,4,5), (7,2,3): No overlap, total 33. (13,5,6), (11,4,5), (9,3,4): Overlap. (13,5,6), (11,4,5), (7,2,3): Overlap at 5-6 and 4-5? Share index 5, invalid. (13,5,6), (9,3,4), (7,2,3): No overlap, total 29. Maximum is 39.

Step 1: Generate all windows of size 1. Windows: (10,0,0), (20,1,1), (30,2,2), (40,3,3), (50,4,4). Step 2: Find top 3 non-overlapping windows. Since k=1, windows are single indices, non-overlapping means different indices. Simply pick the three highest values: 50, 40, 30. Total = 50+40+30 = 120. Check combinations: (50,4,4), (40,3,3), (30,2,2): No overlap, total 120. Any other combination gives lower total.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate and Score All Windows of Size k
ladders.desc: Generate all possible contiguous windows of size k from an array and calculate their congestion scores.
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function for the brute force approach. The intuition is that to find the best windows, we first need to enumerate all possible windows and compute their scores. A window of size k is a contiguous subarray of length k. For an array of length n, there are n-k+1 such windows. The congestion score of a window is simply the sum of all elements within that window. This sub-routine is critical because it creates the pool of candidate windows from which we'll later select three non-overlapping ones. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty list 'windows' to store (score, start_index, end_index) tuples. 2. For start index i from 0 to n-k (inclusive): a. Calculate end index j = i + k - 1. b. Initialize score = 0. c. For index from i to j (inclusive): score += arr[index]. d. Append (score, i, j) to windows list. 3. Return the windows list. Time complexity: O(nk) because for each of O(n) windows, we sum k elements. Space complexity: O(n) to store the window information. Connection to L0: This sub-routine provides the list of scored windows that L0 uses to find the best combination. Connection to L2: This builds on the sliding window concept from L2 but implements the naive version. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in loop boundaries, incorrect score calculation, and not storing enough information (like missing start/end indices needed for overlap checking later). Real code pattern: Use a for loop for start index, then an inner loop or built-in sum function to calculate window score.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=5, k=3, number of windows = 5-3+1 = 3. Step 2: Window 1: start i=0, end j=2, indices 0,1,2, values 1,2,3, score = 1+2+3 = 6. Store (6,0,2). Step 3: Window 2: start i=1, end j=3, indices 1,2,3, values 2,3,4, score = 2+3+4 = 9. Store (9,1,3). Step 4: Window 3: start i=2, end j=4, indices 2,3,4, values 3,4,5, score = 3+4+5 = 12. Store (12,2,4). Step 5: Return list [(6,0,2), (9,1,3), (12,2,4)].

Step 1: n=3, k=2, number of windows = 3-2+1 = 2. Step 2: Window 1: start i=0, end j=1, indices 0,1, values 10,20, score = 10+20 = 30. Store (30,0,1). Step 3: Window 2: start i=1, end j=2, indices 1,2, values 20,30, score = 20+30 = 50. Store (50,1,2). Step 4: Return list [(30,0,1), (50,1,2)].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sliding Window Sum Calculation
ladders.desc: Calculate the sum of elements in a sliding window of fixed size k as it moves through an array.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique for efficiently calculating window scores in the brute force approach. The intuition is that instead of recalculating each window sum from scratch (which would be O(k) per window), we can use a sliding window approach to compute consecutive window sums in O(1) time each after the first window. However, in the brute force O(nk²) approach, we actually use the naive O(nk) method for simplicity, but understanding the sliding window optimization is crucial for more efficient approaches. The core insight is that when the window slides one position to the right, most elements remain the same—only the leftmost element leaves the window and a new rightmost element enters. Therefore, new_window_sum = previous_window_sum - arr[left_index] + arr[right_index]. This reduces the time complexity from O(nk) to O(n) for generating all window sums. The step-by-step algorithm for the optimized version: 1. Calculate sum of first window (indices 0 to k-1). 2. Store this as current_sum. 3. For i from 1 to n-k: a. left_index = i-1, right_index = i+k-1. b. current_sum = current_sum - arr[left_index] + arr[right_index]. c. Store current_sum for window starting at i. Time complexity: O(n) for the optimized version. Space complexity: O(1) extra space. Connection to L1: This technique can optimize the window generation in L1 from O(nk) to O(n). Connection to L3: This builds on the concept of array traversal from L3. Common mistakes include: incorrect index calculations, not handling the first window separately, and off-by-one errors in loop boundaries. Real code pattern: Calculate first window sum with a loop or sum(), then use a for loop to slide the window and update the sum.

examples.trace: Step 1: Calculate first window (indices 0,1,2): sum = 1+2+3 = 6. Store 6. Step 2: Slide window to start at i=1: left_index = 0 (value 1 leaves), right_index = 3 (value 4 enters). New sum = 6 - 1 + 4 = 9. Store 9. Step 3: Slide window to start at i=2: left_index = 1 (value 2 leaves), right_index = 4 (value 5 enters). New sum = 9 - 2 + 5 = 12. Store 12. Step 4: Result list [6,9,12].

Step 1: First window (indices 0,1): sum = 10+20 = 30. Store 30. Step 2: Slide to start at i=1: left_index = 0 (10 leaves), right_index = 2 (30 enters). New sum = 30 - 10 + 30 = 50. Store 50. Step 3: Slide to start at i=2: left_index = 1 (20 leaves), right_index = 3 (40 enters). New sum = 50 - 20 + 40 = 70. Store 70. Step 4: Result [30,50,70].

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal and Subarray Extraction
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array and extract contiguous subarrays of a specified length.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is needed before implementing window operations. The intuition is that to work with windows (contiguous subarrays), we need to be able to traverse the array and extract portions of it. This involves understanding array indexing, loop boundaries, and subarray slicing. A subarray is defined by its start and end indices, and it includes all elements between them. For a window of size k starting at index i, the subarray consists of elements at indices i, i+1, ..., i+k-1. The key is to ensure we don't go out of bounds—the last valid start index is n-k. This building block teaches how to systematically generate all possible subarrays of a fixed length. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty list to store subarrays. 2. For start index i from 0 to n-k (inclusive): a. Initialize an empty list for current subarray. b. For j from i to i+k-1 (inclusive): Append arr[j] to current subarray. c. Append current subarray to result list. 3. Return result list. Time complexity: O(nk) because we create k elements for each of O(n) subarrays. Space complexity: O(nk) to store all subarrays. Connection to L2: This provides the subarrays that L2 will sum to get window scores. Connection to L4: This builds on basic array access operations from L4. Common mistakes include: incorrect loop boundaries causing index out of bounds, off-by-one errors, and misunderstanding inclusive/exclusive ranges. Real code pattern: Use nested loops—outer loop for start index, inner loop to collect k elements.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=4, k=2, last start index = 4-2 = 2. Step 2: i=0: Create subarray from indices 0 to 1: elements arr[0]=1, arr[1]=2 → [1,2]. Step 3: i=1: Create subarray from indices 1 to 2: elements arr[1]=2, arr[2]=3 → [2,3]. Step 4: i=2: Create subarray from indices 2 to 3: elements arr[2]=3, arr[3]=4 → [3,4]. Step 5: Return [[1,2], [2,3], [3,4]].
Step 1: n=3, k=3, last start index = 3-3 = 0. Step 2: i=0: Create subarray from indices 0 to 2: elements arr[0]=10, arr[1]=20, arr[2]=30 → [10,20,30]. Step 3: Return [[10,20,30]].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Access and Summation
ladders.desc: Access individual elements in an array and compute their sum.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used throughout the window congestion problem. The intuition is simple: to calculate a window's congestion score, we need to access each element in the window and add them together. Arrays are zero-indexed data structures where elements are stored at consecutive indices. Accessing an element at index i is typically an O(1) operation. Summation involves initializing a total to 0, then iterating through the elements, adding each one to the total. This is a building block for more complex operations like window sums. The step-by-step algorithm for summing elements from index start to end: 1. Initialize total = 0. 2. For i from start to end (inclusive): a. total = total + arr[i]. 3. Return total. Time complexity: O(m) where m is the number of elements being summed (end-start+1). Space complexity: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used within L3's subarray extraction to calculate sums. Connection to L5: This builds on the concept of arrays from L5. Common mistakes include: incorrect loop boundaries, forgetting to initialize the total, and using wrong indices. Real code pattern: Use a for loop with range(start, end+1) and accumulate sum.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize total = 0. Step 2: i=1: total = 0 + arr[1] = 0 + 2 = 2. Step 3: i=2: total = 2 + arr[2] = 2 + 3 = 5. Step 4: i=3: total = 5 + arr[3] = 5 + 4 = 9. Step 5: Return 9.
Step 1: Initialize total = 0. Step 2: i=0: total = 0 + arr[0] = 0 + 10 = 10. Step 3: i=1: total = 10 + arr[1] = 10 + 20 = 30. Step 4: i=2: total = 30 + arr[2] = 30 + 30 = 60. Step 5: Return 60.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand what an array is and how to access elements using indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept for the entire problem. An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations. Each element has an index (position) starting from 0. The intuition is that arrays allow us to organize data so we can quickly access any element if we know its index. For example, if we have an array arr = [10, 20, 30], then arr[0] = 10, arr[1] = 20, arr[2] = 30. This is crucial because in the congestion problem, the input is an array of congestion values, and we need to access specific elements to calculate window sums. Arrays have fixed size (length) which determines the valid indices (0 to length-1). Trying to access an index outside this range causes an error. This foundation concept involves: 1. Creating an array. 2. Accessing elements by index. 3. Understanding zero-based indexing. 4. Knowing the array length. Time complexity for access: O(1). Space complexity: O(n) to store n elements. Connection to L4: This provides the basic understanding needed for L4's element access operations. Common mistakes include: confusing zero-based with one-based indexing, accessing out-of-bounds indices, and misunderstanding array mutability. Real code pattern: In Python, arrays are typically represented as lists: arr = [1,2,3]; element = arr[0].

examples.trace: Step 1: Array has 4 elements: indices 0,1,2,3. Step 2: Index 2 refers to the third element (since counting starts at 0). Step 3: arr[2] = 15. Step 4: Return 15.
Step 1: Array has 3 elements: indices 0,1,2. Step 2: Index 0 refers to the first element. Step 3: arr[0] = 100. Step 4: Return 100.

approaches.name: Heap-based Three Pass Selection O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Precompute window scores using sliding window, then use a max-heap to select top windows in three passes while marking selected windows and their neighbors as unavailable. This greedy-like approach efficiently finds high-scoring non-overlapping windows.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Heap-based Three Pass Selection for Top Three Non-overlapping Windows
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find top three non-overlapping windows of size k with highest congestion scores using sliding window and max-heap.
ladders.explanation: This solution addresses the core challenge of selecting high-scoring windows without overlaps. The intuition is that we need to greedily pick the best available windows while respecting non-overlap constraints. First, we precompute congestion scores for all windows of size k using a sliding window technique, which is efficient. Then, we use a max-heap to select windows in three passes: each pass extracts the highest-scoring window from the heap, adds it to the result, and marks it and its overlapping neighbors as unavailable. This greedy approach works because the heap ensures we always pick the best remaining window, and marking neighbors prevents overlaps. The three passes guarantee we get exactly three windows if available. Time complexity is O(n log n) due to heap operations on up to n windows, and space is O(n) for storing scores and heap. Connection to L1: This integrates the key subroutine of heap-based selection with neighbor marking. Common mistakes include not properly handling edge cases (e.g., fewer than three windows) or incorrect neighbor marking logic, which can be avoided by carefully defining overlap conditions and testing with varied inputs. Real code pattern: Precompute scores, build max-heap of (score, index) pairs, initialize result list and unavailable set, then loop three times: while heap not empty, pop max, if index not unavailable, add to result, mark index and neighbors as unavailable, break if three windows found.

examples.trace: Step 1: Precompute window scores for size k=2: windows are indices 0-1: score 3+1=4, 1-2: 1+4=5, 2-3: 4+2=6, 3-4: 2+5=7. Scores: [(4,0), (5,1), (6,2), (7,3)] where tuple is (score, start_index). Step 2: Build max-heap: [(7,3), (6,2), (5,1), (4,0)]. Step 3: First pass: pop (7,3), add to result, mark index 3 and neighbors (2,4) as unavailable (since windows overlap if start indices differ by less than k=2). Unavailable set: {2,3,4}. Step 4: Second pass: pop (6,2), index 2 is unavailable, skip. Pop (5,1), index 1 not unavailable, add to result, mark index 1 and neighbors (0,2) as unavailable. Unavailable set: {0,1,2,3,4}. Step 5: Third pass: heap now [(4,0)], pop (4,0), index 0 is unavailable, skip. Heap empty, but we only have 2 windows in result. Need to continue: since only 2 windows found after processing heap, we stop. Result: [(7,3), (5,1)] but sorted by score descending: [(7,3), (5,1)]. Wait, we need top three: only two windows exist without overlap? Let's check: windows are (score, start): 7 at index 3, 6 at 2, 5 at 1, 4 at 0. Non-overlapping sets: pick 7 (index 3), then can pick 5 (index 1) or 4 (index 0), but not 6 (overlaps with 7). So top three non-overlapping: 7,5,4. But our algorithm gave only two because after picking 7 and 5, all indices are unavailable. Actually, after marking neighbors for 7: indices 2,3,4 unavailable. For 5: indices 0,1,2 unavailable. So unavailable set is all indices, no third window possible. Thus output should be [(7,3), (5,1)] only. But problem says find top three, so if less than three, return what we have. So output: [(7,3), (5,1)].

Step 1: Precompute scores for k=3: windows: index 0-2: 10+20+30=60, 1-3: 20+30+40=90, 2-4: 30+40+50=120. Scores: [(60,0), (90,1), (120,2)]. Step 2: Max-heap: [(120,2), (90,1), (60,0)]. Step 3: First pass: pop (120,2), add to result, mark index 2 and neighbors (0,1,3,4) as unavailable? Overlap if start indices differ by less than k=3, so neighbors are indices 0,1,3,4? Actually, for index 2, windows that overlap have start indices in [2-2, 2+2] = [0,4] since k=3, but we only have indices 0,1,2. So mark 0,1,2,3,4? Better: mark index 2 and indices within k-1=2 distance: i.e., indices 0,1,2,3,4. But we only have windows at 0,1,2. So unavailable set: {0,1,2,3,4}. Step 4: Second pass: heap has [(90,1), (60,0)], pop (90,1), index 1 is unavailable, skip. Pop (60,0), index 0 unavailable, skip. Heap empty, result has one window. So output: [(120,2)] only. But we can pick non-overlapping windows: 120 at index 2 and 60 at index 0 do not overlap? Check: index 0 window covers 0-2, index 2 covers 2-4, they overlap at index 2, so they overlap. Thus only one window can be selected. Output: [(120,2)].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Heap-based Selection with Neighbor Marking
ladders.desc: Select top m non-overlapping items from a list using a max-heap and marking neighbors as unavailable.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the full problem, focusing on how to greedily pick high-value items without overlaps. The intuition is that by using a max-heap, we can efficiently access the highest-scoring item, and by marking its neighbors as unavailable, we enforce non-overlap constraints. This mimics a greedy algorithm that works well for interval scheduling problems. Steps: 1. Build a max-heap from items with their scores and indices. 2. Initialize an empty result list and a set to track unavailable indices. 3. For up to m iterations (e.g., m=3): a. While heap is not empty, pop the top item. b. If its index is not in the unavailable set, add it to the result. c. Mark this index and all overlapping indices (based on a given overlap distance) as unavailable. d. Break if we have m items. 4. Return the result. Time complexity is O(n log n) for heap operations, where n is the number of items, and space is O(n) for the heap and set. Connection to L0: This sub-routine is called after precomputing window scores to select the top three windows. Common mistakes include incorrect overlap marking (e.g., not marking enough neighbors) or not handling cases where fewer than m items are available, which can be avoided by defining overlap rules clearly and checking heap emptiness. Real code pattern: Use heapq in Python for max-heap (with negative scores), and maintain a set for unavailable indices.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build max-heap: [(40,3), (30,2), (20,1), (10,0)]. Step 2: Result=[], unavailable={}. Step 3: First pass: pop (40,3), index 3 not unavailable, add to result: [(40,3)]. Mark index 3 and neighbors within overlap_dist=1: indices 2,3,4. Unavailable={2,3,4}. Step 4: Second pass: pop (30,2), index 2 is unavailable, skip. Pop (20,1), index 1 not unavailable, add to result: [(40,3), (20,1)]. Mark index 1 and neighbors: indices 0,1,2. Unavailable={0,1,2,3,4}. Step 5: Have m=2 items, stop. Output: [(40,3), (20,1)].

Step 1: Max-heap: [(5,2), (5,1), (5,0)] (ties broken by index, but heap order may vary; assume sorted). Step 2: Result=[], unavailable={}. Step 3: First pass: pop (5,2), add to result: [(5,2)]. Mark index 2 and neighbors within 2: indices 0,1,2,3,4. Unavailable={0,1,2,3,4}. Step 4: Second pass: heap has [(5,1), (5,0)], pop (5,1), index 1 unavailable, skip. Pop (5,0), index 0 unavailable, skip. Heap empty, result has one item. Continue: need m=3, but only one item available due to overlaps. So output: [(5,2)] only. But wait, with overlap_dist=2, picking index 2 makes all indices unavailable, so only one can be picked. Thus output: [(5,2)].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Selection with Constraint Enforcement via Marking
ladders.desc: The technique of iteratively picking the best available option while invalidating conflicting options.
ladders.explanation: This core logic underpins many optimization problems where we need to select items under constraints. The intuition is that by always choosing the highest-value item and then eliminating overlapping choices, we can efficiently approximate or achieve optimal solutions, especially in interval scheduling or resource allocation. This greedy approach works when the constraint is that selected items cannot overlap, and it often yields good results if not always optimal, but in this problem, it's designed to find top m windows. Steps: 1. Maintain a collection of items sorted by value (e.g., using a heap). 2. Repeatedly select the highest-value item that is still available. 3. After selection, mark this item and all items that conflict with it (based on a defined conflict rule, such as overlap in indices) as unavailable. 4. Continue until m items are selected or no items remain. Time complexity depends on the data structure; with a heap, it's O(n log n) for n items. Space is O(n) for tracking availability. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the sub-routine using a heap and a set for marking. Common mistakes include not correctly defining conflict rules or not updating availability efficiently, which can lead to incorrect selections or inefficiency. Real code pattern: Use a priority queue and a boolean array or set to mark conflicts.

examples.input: items = [('A',10), ('B',8), ('C',6), ('D',4)], conflicts: A conflicts with B, C; B conflicts with A, C; C conflicts with A, B, D; D conflicts with C. Select top 2.
examples.trace: Step 1: Sort by value: [('A',10), ('B',8), ('C',6), ('D',4)]. Step 2: Select A (value 10), mark A and its conflicts B,C as unavailable. Available: D. Step 3: Select next highest available: D (value 4), mark D and its conflict C as unavailable (C already unavailable). Step 4: Have 2 items, stop. Output: [('A',10), ('D',4)].

examples.input: items = [('X',5), ('Y',5), ('Z',5)], conflicts: X conflicts with Y; Y conflicts with X, Z; Z conflicts with Y. Select top 3.
examples.trace: Step 1: Items all value 5, assume order X,Y,Z. Step 2: Select X, mark X and conflict Y as unavailable. Available: Z. Step 3: Select Z, mark Z and conflict Y as unavailable (Y already unavailable). Step 4: Need top 3, but only 2 available due to conflicts. Output: [('X',5), ('Z',5)].

testCases.input: items = [('P',100), ('Q',90), ('R',80)], conflicts: P conflicts with Q; Q conflicts with P,R; R conflicts with Q. Select top 2.
items = [('M',7), ('N',6), ('O',5)], conflicts: M conflicts with N; N conflicts with M,O; O conflicts with N. Select top 1.
items = [('S',3), ('T',3), ('U',3)], conflicts: S conflicts with T; T conflicts with S,U; U conflicts with T. Select top 3.
items = [('V',10), ('W',9)], conflicts: V conflicts with W; W conflicts with V. Select top 2.
items = [('A',5), ('B',4), ('C',3)], conflicts: A conflicts with B,C; B conflicts with A; C conflicts with A. Select top 2.
items = [('F',6), ('G',5), ('H',4), ('I',3)], conflicts: F conflicts with G; G conflicts with F,H; H conflicts with G,I; I conflicts with H. Select top 3.
items = [('J',8), ('K',7), ('L',6)], conflicts: J conflicts with K,L; K conflicts with J; L conflicts with J. Select top 2.
items = [('X',9), ('Y',8), ('Z',7)], conflicts: X conflicts with Y; Y conflicts with X; Z conflicts with none. Select top 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Sliding Window Sum Computation
ladders.desc: Compute the sum of all contiguous subarrays of fixed size k in an array.
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential for precomputing congestion scores for each window. The intuition is that instead of recomputing sums from scratch for each window, we can slide a window across the array and update the sum efficiently by adding the new element and subtracting the old one. This reduces time complexity from O(n*k) to O(n). Steps: 1. Calculate the sum of the first k elements. 2. Store this as the score for the first window (starting at index 0). 3. For each subsequent window from index 1 to n-k: a. Subtract the element that slides out (at index i-1). b. Add the element that slides in (at index i+k-1). c. Store the sum for the current window. Time complexity is O(n) for n elements, and space is O(n) if storing all window sums. Connection to L2: This provides the item values (scores) for the greedy selection logic. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices or not handling cases where k > n, which can be avoided by initializing correctly and adding bounds checks. Real code pattern: Use a loop to compute initial sum, then iterate while updating sum.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initial sum for first window (indices 0-2): 1+2+3 = 6. Step 2: Slide to window starting at index 1: subtract arr[0]=1, add arr[3]=4, new sum = 6 - 1 + 4 = 9. Step 3: Slide to window starting at index 2: subtract arr[1]=2, add arr[4]=5, new sum = 9 - 2 + 5 = 12. Step 4: No more windows (n=5, k=3, last start index is 2). Output: [6, 9, 12].
Step 1: Initial sum for window 0-1: 10+20 = 30. Step 2: Slide to window starting at index 1: subtract arr[0]=10, add arr[2]=30, new sum = 30 - 10 + 30 = 50. Step 3: Output: [30, 50].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Max-Heap Insertion and Extraction
ladders.desc: Insert elements into a max-heap and extract the maximum element efficiently.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is fundamental for maintaining a collection where we need frequent access to the largest element. A max-heap is a complete binary tree where each node is greater than or equal to its children, allowing O(log n) insertion and extraction. Intuition: By organizing data in a heap, we can quickly retrieve the max value without sorting the entire list each time. Steps: For insertion: 1. Add the new element at the end of the heap. 2. Bubble it up by comparing with its parent and swapping if larger, until heap property is restored. For extraction: 1. Remove the root (max element). 2. Move the last element to the root. 3. Bubble it down by comparing with children and swapping with the larger child, until heap property is restored. Time complexity is O(log n) per operation for a heap of size n, and space is O(n). Connection to L3: This operation is used to manage window scores for greedy selection. Common mistakes include incorrect comparison logic or not handling heapify after operations, which can be avoided by implementing standard heap algorithms. Real code pattern: In Python, use heapq module with negative values for max-heap, or implement manually with list and index calculations.

examples.input: Insert into empty max-heap: 5, 3, 8, 1, then extract max.
examples.trace: Step 1: Insert 5: heap [5]. Step 2: Insert 3: add at end [5,3], bubble up: 3 <= 5, no swap. Heap [5,3]. Step 3: Insert 8: add at end [5,3,8], bubble up: compare 8 with parent 5, swap -> [8,3,5]. Step 4: Insert 1: add at end [8,3,5,1], bubble up: 1 <= parent 5, no swap. Heap [8,3,5,1]. Step 5: Extract max: remove root 8, move last element 1 to root [1,3,5], bubble down: compare 1 with children 3 and 5, swap with larger child 5 -> [5,3,1]. Output: extracted 8, heap [5,3,1].

Step 1: Initial heap [10,7,9,5] (assumed max-heap property). Step 2: Extract max: remove 10, move last 5 to root [5,7,9], bubble down: compare 5 with children 7 and 9, swap with larger 9 -> [9,7,5]. Output first extract: 10. Step 3: Extract max from [9,7,5]: remove 9, move last 5 to root [5,7], bubble down: compare 5 with child 7, swap -> [7,5]. Output second extract: 9.

testCases.input: Insert into empty: 4, 2, 6, then extract max.
Insert into empty: 1, 2, 3, 4, extract max twice.
Insert into empty: 7, 3, 9, 1, extract max, then insert 8.
Heap [12, 11, 10], extract max, insert 13, extract max.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array and Contiguous Subarrays
ladders.desc: Understand arrays and how to access contiguous sequences of elements.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite, focusing on basic array operations. An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, allowing direct access via indices. Contiguous subarrays are sequences of elements that appear consecutively in the array. Intuition: To solve window-based problems, we need to consider all possible contiguous subarrays of a given size, which forms the basis for computing scores. Steps: 1. Define an array with n elements indexed from 0 to n-1. 2. A subarray of size k starting at index i includes elements from i to i+k-1. 3. To access these elements, use a loop from i to i+k-1. 4. The total number of such subarrays is n-k+1 (if k <= n). Time complexity for accessing one subarray is O(k), and space is O(1) if not storing. Connection to L4: This concept is used to define windows for sum computation. Common mistakes include incorrect index bounds or off-by-one errors, which can be avoided by careful loop conditions and testing with small examples. Real code pattern: Use for loops to iterate through start indices and inner loops to access elements.

examples.input: Array [10, 20, 30, 40], list all contiguous subarrays of size 2.
examples.trace: Step 1: Array has indices 0,1,2,3. Size k=2. Step 2: Start at i=0: subarray includes indices 0 and 1: [10,20]. Step 3: Start at i=1: indices 1 and 2: [20,30]. Step 4: Start at i=2: indices 2 and 3: [30,40]. Step 5: No more starts since i=3 would go beyond array (3+2-1=4 > 3). Output: [[10,20], [20,30], [30,40]].

examples.input: Array [5, 6, 7], list all contiguous subarrays of size 3.
examples.trace: Step 1: Array indices 0,1,2, k=3. Step 2: Start at i=0: subarray indices 0,1,2: [5,6,7]. Step 3: Next start i=1 would give indices 1,2,3, but index 3 doesn't exist. So only one subarray. Output: [[5,6,7]].
